description: Orthopedic consultation note — acute right knee injury, suspected meniscal tear, requested by primary care physician
parsed_text:
ORTHOPEDIC SURGERY CONSULTATION NOTE
Summit Orthopedics & Sports Medicine
Department: Orthopedic Surgery
Encounter ID: SOSM-CONS-2025-007831
Date: 2025-10-30

Consulting Provider: Dr. James Calloway, MD
NPI: 4455667788
Specialty: Orthopedic Surgery

Patient: Nguyen, Linh T.
DOB: 1992-06-14
MRN: SOSM-MRN-00114522

Referring Provider: Dr. Angela Torres, MD (Family Medicine)
Facility: Summit Orthopedics & Sports Medicine

---
CHIEF COMPLAINT
Right knee pain and swelling after soccer injury 5 days ago, unable to fully extend knee.

SUBJECTIVE
33-year-old active female presents for orthopedic consultation after right knee injury during recreational soccer game on 10/25/2025. She reports a twisting mechanism of injury with immediate onset of pain, swelling, and locking sensation. Pain rated 6/10 at rest, 9/10 with ambulation. Unable to fully extend right knee. Ice and ibuprofen have provided partial relief. No prior knee injuries or surgeries. No other joint complaints.

OBJECTIVE
Vitals:
  BP: 118/76 mmHg   HR: 68 bpm   Temp: 98.2°F   RR: 14 breaths/min
  SpO2: 99% on room air   Weight: 143 lbs

Physical Exam — Right Knee:
  Moderate effusion present. Flexion 0-115° (limited by pain). Locked at -15° extension.
  McMurray test: Positive for medial meniscus pain with external rotation.
  Lachman test: Negative. Valgus/varus stress: Stable.
  Neurovascular exam: Intact distally.

Imaging: X-rays (right knee, AP/lateral/sunrise views) reviewed — no fracture, joint space preserved.
MRI right knee ordered (results pending).

ASSESSMENT
1. Suspected medial meniscus tear, right knee (M23.201) — Positive McMurray, effusion, locked extension deficit. Awaiting MRI confirmation.
2. Right knee joint effusion (M25.361) — Moderate, likely hemarthrosis or reactive.

PLAN
1. MRI right knee without contrast — ordered STAT, scheduled for 11/01/2025.
2. Aspiration of right knee joint effusion if clinically indicated after MRI.
3. Knee immobilizer placed. Crutches provided, weight-bearing as tolerated.
4. Continue ibuprofen 600 mg TID with food. Ice 20 min every 4 hours.
5. If MRI confirms meniscal tear: discuss arthroscopic partial meniscectomy vs. conservative management based on tear pattern.
6. Return to clinic in 7 days to review MRI results and discuss treatment plan.

Electronically signed: Dr. James Calloway, MD — 2025-10-30 16:05
